sourceText: Appointed Archbishop of Thessalonica by Emperor Theophilos.
Deposed as Archbishop following the official end of Byzantine Iconoclasm.
His fame reaches the Abbasid Caliph, who invites him to Baghdad. Leo refuses, gaining favor with the Byzantine Emperor.
Appointed head (scholarch) of the newly re-founded Magnaura School by the regent Bardas.
after 869
Last known historical mention of him. His exact death date is unknown.
Revival of Classical Learning
He systematically collected and copied ancient Greek manuscripts, preserving works of mathematics, philosophy, and science.
Leadership of the Magnaura School
As head of the imperial university, he educated a new generation of scholars, sparking the Macedonian Renaissance.
Invention of a Communication System
He is credited with inventing a system of beacons, an optical telegraph, that stretched across Anatolia to warn of Arab raids.
Why is he called 'the Mathematician'?
While a polymath, his exceptional skill in mathematics and logic was his most defining trait, earning him the title. His work involved algebra and the study of classical Greek mathematicians.
Did he really turn down an offer from the Caliph?

Yes, historical accounts state that the Abbasid Caliph, impressed by his knowledge, offered him immense wealth to come to Baghdad. Leo, encouraged by the Byzantine Emperor, chose to remain in Constantinople.
What was the Macedonian Renaissance?
It was a 9th and 10th-century cultural revival in the Byzantine Empire, marked by a renewed interest in classical Greek scholarship, art, and literature. Leo was a central figure in this movement.
Born as Ibrahim Qunanbaiuly in the Chingis Mountains, modern-day Kazakhstan.
Attended a madrasa in Semipalatinsk, followed by a Russian school, giving him a unique dual education.
Began to write poetry with serious dedication, adopting the pen name 'Abai'.
Wrote his major philosophical work, the 'Book of Words' (Kara Sozder).
Suffered the death of his son, Abdirakhman.
Died in the Zhidebay valley, shortly after the death of his other son, Magauiya.
Founder of Modern Kazakh Literature
He reformed the Kazakh literary language, introducing new poetic forms, genres, and themes, effectively creating its modern written form.
The 'Book of Words' (Kara Sozder)
A collection of 45 philosophical prose pieces and parables critiquing Kazakh society and advocating for education, morality, and reform.
Cultural Bridge
He translated works by Russian and European authors like Pushkin, Lermontov, and Goethe, introducing new ideas to the Kazakh people.
Composer
He composed over two dozen melodies for his own poems, which have become an integral part of Kazakh folk music.
Why is he called Abai?
'Abai' (meaning 'careful' or 'attentive') was a nickname given to him by his grandmother, Zere, which he later adopted as his pen name. His birth name was Ibrahim.
What are the 'Words of Edification'?
Also known as the 'Book of Words' (Kara Sozder), it is his magnum opus, a collection of 45 philosophical reflections on Kazakh culture, morality, law, and the future of his people.
Was Abai only a writer?
No, Abai was also a gifted composer. He composed over two dozen melodies, many of which set his own poems to music and have become classic Kazakh folk songs.
What was his view on Russia?

Abai had a complex view. He was critical of Tsarist colonial policy but admired Russian literature and culture, seeing it as a source of enlightenment and progress that could benefit the Kazakh people if approached selectively.
Born in the village of Grigorovo, Nizhny Novgorod region, into a priestly family.
Begins his public opposition to the liturgical reforms initiated by Patriarch Nikon of Moscow.
Arrested and exiled to Siberia with his family. Endures a decade of extreme hardship and travel.
Briefly returns to Moscow but refuses to compromise, leading to his re-arrest and exile to the Russian North.
Defrocked and anathematized by the Great Moscow Synod. Imprisoned in an underground pit in Pustozersk.
Writes his autobiography, 'The Life of Avvakum, Written by Himself', a foundational work of Russian literature.
Burned at the stake in Pustozersk along with his closest followers for 'great blasphemies against the Tsar'.
Leader of the Old Believers
Became the most prominent and unyielding leader of the Raskol (schism), galvanizing opposition to the state-backed church reforms.
Author of 'The Life of Avvakum'
Wrote the first major Russian autobiography, a masterpiece of 17th-century literature known for its vivid, colloquial language and passionate defense of his faith.
What were the church reforms Avvakum opposed?

Patriarch Nikon's reforms aimed to align Russian Orthodox practices with the Greek Orthodox Church. This included changing the sign of the cross from two fingers to three, altering the spelling of Jesus's name ('Isus' to 'Iisus'), and revising liturgical books. Avvakum and the Old Believers saw these changes as heresy.
Why is his autobiography so important?

It was one of the first major works written in colloquial, everyday Russian rather than the formal Church Slavonic. Its raw, personal, and passionate style broke new ground in Russian literature and provided a powerful testament to the Old Believer cause.
Was Avvakum a saint?

He is not recognized as a saint by the mainstream Russian Orthodox Church. However, he is venerated as a holy martyr and saint by many denominations of the Old Believers, who see him as the ultimate defender of the true faith.
Becomes co-ruler of the Huns with his brother Bleda.
Murders Bleda to become the sole ruler of the Hunnic Empire.
Launches a major invasion of the Balkans and threatens Constantinople.
Invades Gaul and fights the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains.
Invades Italy, sacks several cities, but spares Rome.
Dies on his wedding night; his empire collapses shortly after.
Unification of the Hunnic Tribes
He united various Hunnic and Germanic tribes into a formidable, centralized fighting force under his sole command.
Subjugation of the Eastern Roman Empire
He repeatedly defeated the Eastern Roman armies, forcing them to pay enormous annual tributes in gold.
Creation of a Vast European Empire
He established a massive, though short-lived, empire stretching from the Ural River to the Rhine and from the Danube to the Baltic Sea.
Why is he called the 'Scourge of God'?
Christian writers in Europe gave him this name, believing his devastating campaigns were a divine punishment for their sins.
Why didn't he conquer Rome in 452?
Historians debate this. Reasons may include a plea from Pope Leo I, disease and famine in his army, or the threat of an Eastern Roman attack on his homeland.
How did Attila die?
He died in 453 on the night of his wedding to a new bride named Ildico. The most accepted cause is a severe nosebleed or internal bleeding, possibly exacerbated by heavy drinking.
Born in Sayram, now in Kazakhstan.
Travels to Bukhara to study under the renowned Sufi master Yusuf Hamadani.
Briefly becomes the head of Hamadani's khaneqah (Sufi lodge) before returning to his hometown of Yasi.
At age 63, in mourning for the Prophet Muhammad, he retreats to an underground cell (chilla-khana) to live out his life in contemplation.
Dies in the city of Yasi (present-day Turkistan, Kazakhstan), where his mausoleum now stands.
Diwan-i-Hikmet (Book of Wisdom)
Authored a collection of poems in the Turkic language, making complex Sufi philosophy accessible to the common people of Central Asia.
Founder of the Yasawiyya Order
Established the first major Turkic Sufi order, which spread his teachings widely across the Turkic-speaking world for centuries.
Pioneer of Turkic Religious Literature
Elevated the status of the Turkic language as a vehicle for profound religious and mystical expression, influencing centuries of literature.
Why is Ahmad Yasawi so important?
He was the first major mystic to compose poetry in a Turkic language, which allowed Islamic and Sufi ideas to spread widely among the nomadic and settled Turkic peoples of Central Asia.
What is the 'Diwan-i-Hikmet'?
It is his collection of poems, called 'hikmats' (wisdoms), which explain the principles of Sufism, ethics, and love for God in a simple, accessible style using Turkic folk-poetry meters.
Why did he go underground at age 63?

He did so out of profound respect and mourning for the Prophet Muhammad, who died at the age of 63. Yasawi felt it was improper for him to continue living above ground and see a sun the Prophet could no longer see.
Born in the Porphyra chamber of the Great Palace of Constantinople, as the first child of Emperor Alexios I Komnenos and Irene Doukaina.
As a teenager, she witnesses the arrival of the leaders of the First Crusade in Constantinople, an event she would later chronicle in detail.

Her father, Alexios I, dies. She and her mother attempt to persuade him to name her husband, Nikephoros Bryennios, as his successor, but fail. She then conspires against her brother, the new Emperor John II.
After her plot is discovered, she is stripped of her property and exiled to the Kecharitomene Monastery, which her mother had founded.
Following the death of her husband, she begins writing the Alexiad, a 15-book history of her father's reign, likely completing a work her husband had started.
Dies in the monastery, having completed her monumental historical work.
Author of the Alexiad
She wrote the Alexiad, a 15-book historical text that is one of the most important primary sources for the history of the Byzantine Empire and the First Crusade.
Pioneering Female Historian
She is widely regarded as one of the first and most significant female historians in the Western world, setting a precedent for female scholarship.
Medical Administrator and Scholar
She possessed extensive medical knowledge, administered a large hospital and orphanage in Constantinople, and wrote about diseases and treatments in the Alexiad.
Was Anna Komnene really supposed to be Empress?

As the firstborn child, she was initially the designated heir and was betrothed to the co-emperor, placing her in the direct line of succession. Her status changed after her brother John was born and later designated as heir by their father.
What is the Alexiad about?

It is a detailed account of the political and military history of the Byzantine Empire during the reign of her father, Alexios I Komnenos, from 1081 to 1118. It also provides a unique Byzantine perspective on the First Crusade.
Is the Alexiad a reliable historical source?

While it is an invaluable firsthand account, it is also highly biased. Anna glorifies her father and family while often criticizing or downplaying their rivals. Historians use it critically, cross-referencing it with Latin, Armenian, and other sources to get a more balanced view.
Born, likely in the Principality of Moscow, during the era of the Tatar Yoke.
Collaborated with Theophanes the Greek and Prokhor of Gorodets on the frescoes for the Cathedral of the Annunciation in the Moscow Kremlin.
Painted frescoes and the iconostasis in the Dormition Cathedral in Vladimir, alongside his friend and collaborator Daniil Chyorny.
